title: The Whale

jokes.text: That Swayze sure can dance dirty.
jokes.setup: Andy mentions seeing Dirty Dancing 10 times and being emotional about it
jokes.punchline: Reduces Patrick Swayze's acting/dancing prowess to simply 'dancing dirty'
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay on the movie title, delivered with Andy's characteristic oblivious earnestness

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've seen Dirty Dancing like ten times. It just gets me every time.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Patrick Swayze was just... he could dance dirty.

jokes.text: They're like two flaming meatballs in my skull.
jokes.setup: Andy complaining about sun exposure affecting his eyes
jokes.punchline: Grotesque and oddly specific comparison to meatballs
jokes.explanation: Vivid, original imagery that's both disgusting and funny. Perfect Andy-ism with great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: My eyes are really sensitive to the sun. It's like looking directly at two meatballs that have been left out in the sun for three days.

jokes.text: No, Woman. No Cry.
jokes.setup: Andy explains he was sad about being left behind but remembered a Bob Marley song
jokes.punchline: Completely butchers the song title, missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Andy's cultural cluelessness and tendency to mishear things

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, I was really sad about being left behind, but then I remembered this Bob Marley song that really spoke to me.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: "Don't Worry, Be Happy."

jokes.text: also helps me combat the glare bear. That's what I call the sun now.
jokes.setup: Andy shows off his makeshift hat made from underwear
jokes.punchline: Childish nickname for the sun delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Absurd but memorable phrase that feels authentically Andy. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I call it a 'ballcap.'

jokes.text: Andy, it's Darryl. Take your drawers off your head.
jokes.setup: Andy has been wearing underwear as a hat
jokes.punchline: Darryl's matter-of-fact instruction about the absurd situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man reaction. The casualness makes the absurdity funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Man, take the underwear off your head.

jokes.text: Ow! Oh! Oh, no. Ah. That's not good.
jokes.setup: Andy demonstrates his desalinator device
jokes.punchline: Device clearly hurts him, delivered with understated concern
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy setup with Andy's typical understated reaction to disaster

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've created a desalinator. It takes salt water and makes it fresh. Here, let me show you.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Ow!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's working.

jokes.text: Andy falling overboard during dramatic speech
jokes.setup: Andy gives poetic farewell speech about 'man and his boat'
jokes.punchline: Immediately falls overboard, undermining his maritime competence
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic timing - the pretentious speech immediately undercut by incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Man and his boat, they're intertwined. The boat is an extension of man's soul, his dreams, his aspirations...

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Out here on the water, we're not just men in a boat. We're philosophers, we're adventurers, we're—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: *falls overboard*

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Help! Help! I'm drowning!

jokes.text: Why would you ever call me when the manager's out of town?
jokes.setup: David Wallace calls the office
jokes.punchline: Dwight's immediate assumption that he shouldn't be the contact
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight - simultaneously insecure and presumptuous about his importance

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: Hi, this is David Wallace calling for whoever's in charge.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This is Dwight Schrute, Assistant Regional Manager.

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: Actually, I need to speak with Michael Scott.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Michael is unavailable. I can help you with whatever you need.

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: No, I really need Michael specifically.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Of course you do.

jokes.text: You didn't call Jim. That seems significant.
jokes.setup: David Wallace has called with big news
jokes.punchline: Dwight immediately makes it about his rivalry with Jim
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight behavior - turning professional moment into petty competition

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What is it, David? Did we get the Sanderson account? Please tell me we got the Sanderson account.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jim didn't get it, did he? Because if Jim got it, I swear to God...

jokes.text: Do you want it? No. Do you use it? No. Does it inexplicably show up on your doorstep three times a year? Yes, yes, and yes.
jokes.setup: David explains the White Pages opportunity
jokes.punchline: Perfect description of everyone's relationship with phone books
jokes.explanation: Perfectly structured observational humor about a universal experience. Great rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: The White Pages is a perfect description of everyone's relationship with phone books.

jokes.text: There is a reason we in the paper industry call this thing 'the white whale.'
jokes.setup: Building up the importance of the White Pages account
jokes.punchline: Literary reference that also plays on 'white pages' being literally white
jokes.explanation: Clever literary reference that works on multiple levels - both prestigious and literal

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: The White Pages account is one of the most important accounts we have. Do you know what that means? It means we are in the same league as Moby Dick.

jokes.text: Look at all that sweet blubber.
jokes.setup: Following up on the white whale metaphor
jokes.punchline: Dwight takes the whale metaphor literally
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight moment - taking metaphor literally with characteristic intensity

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about what you said about the white whale. In Moby Dick, Ahab was obsessed with hunting it. He let that obsession consume him.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Right, so what's your point?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My point is, I've decided to hunt a white whale. I've already contacted several maritime organizations. I'm leaving next Monday.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, no—

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've purchased a harpoon. A real one. Not a toy. And I'm naming it Bessie.

jokes.text: Eat it, Jim! Eat it, Phyllis! Eat-- Where's Stanley?
jokes.setup: Dwight celebrating being chosen for the big account
jokes.punchline: Can't find Stanley to gloat to, showing his need to rub it in everyone's face
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight pettiness - must gloat to everyone individually, even tracking down Stanley

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have been chosen for the Kline account. Finally, my superior intellect and sales acumen have been recognized.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Now where is Stanley? I need to tell him about this immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Stanley! STANLEY! I need to rub my success directly in your face!

jokes.text: Gina Rogers at Apex Technology said you called her 'gy-na' for your entire meeting.
jokes.setup: Jim explaining why Dwight can't handle women clients
jokes.punchline: Dwight's mispronunciation of 'Gina' sounds like slang for vagina
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy - Dwight's social awkwardness creates unintentionally crude situation

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight can't handle women clients because every time he tries to say 'Gina,' it comes out sounding like... well, let's just say it's not great for business.

jokes.text: Gy-na said that?
jokes.setup: Jim just explained Dwight's mispronunciation problem
jokes.punchline: Dwight immediately does it again, showing he doesn't understand the issue
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that shows Dwight completely missed the point. Maximum cringe efficiency.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I pronounce all words correctly. For instance, I say 'nuclear' not 'nucular.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, but you just said 'nucular.'

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No, I said 'nuclear.' You're not listening properly.

jokes.text: Hey, remember that week in the '90s when we got bagels?
jokes.setup: Discussion of potential perks from big sale
jokes.punchline: Kevin's nostalgic memory of a single week of bagels decades ago
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kevin line - simple pleasures remembered with disproportionate fondness

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You know, we had bagels here once. For like a week. Back in 2003. Best week of my life.

jokes.text: I miss Clinton.
jokes.setup: Kevin's nostalgia for '90s bagels
jokes.punchline: Random political nostalgia tied to office snacks
jokes.explanation: Perfectly random Kevin non-sequitur that somehow makes sense for the character

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You know, back in the '90s, bagels just tasted better. They were simpler times.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Kevin, bagels are the same.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Clinton was president, and the bagels reflected that. They had hope in them.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's not how bagels work.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: When Bush came in, the bagels got stale. It was like the country's bagel morale just dropped.

jokes.text: I hope you like being turned on all the time.
jokes.setup: Explaining the Movember mustaches to camera
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate sexual comment about their appearance
jokes.explanation: Typical Packer inappropriateness. In character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: Inappropriate sexual comment about their appearance

jokes.text: I have very fertile hair glands.
jokes.setup: Discussion of mustache growth
jokes.punchline: Creed's weird pseudo-scientific explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed weirdness - sounds medical but makes no sense. Classic absurdist character humor.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: You know, I've been growing this mustache for three weeks. It's not just hair, it's a statement. A statement that says I don't believe in the rules of society. Also, I read that if you grow a mustache, it adds ten years to your life. I'm gonna live forever.

jokes.text: Smile if you love men's prostates.
jokes.setup: Nate and the warehouse guys picking up women while fundraising
jokes.punchline: Terrible pickup line about prostate cancer
jokes.explanation: Hilariously inappropriate pickup line that shows complete social cluelessness

dialogue.speaker: Nate
dialogue.line: Hey baby, are you a prostate exam? Because I've been waiting for you to come around for years.

jokes.text: I think the Senator is having an affair.
jokes.setup: Angela doing spring cleaning
jokes.punchline: Drops this bomb casually while organizing
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Oscar's panic. Angela's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm doing some spring cleaning and I found Sprinkles' remains in the back of the closet. I didn't have the heart to tell Dwight, so I just threw them away and got a new cat that looks identical.

jokes.text: I literally have nightmares in which what just happened happens.
jokes.setup: Angela casually mentions Senator having affair
jokes.punchline: Oscar's specific fear coming true
jokes.explanation: Perfect Oscar neurosis - he's literally dreaded this exact conversation happening

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Senator has been having an affair.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: With a man?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: My worst nightmare.

jokes.text: And then I make Angela's husband spoon me back to bed.
jokes.setup: Oscar describing his nightmares about this situation
jokes.punchline: Reveals the uncomfortable sleeping arrangement
jokes.explanation: Dark and uncomfortable revelation that perfectly captures the triangle's dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: I've been having nightmares about this. Last night I dreamed that I was sleeping in the same bed as Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: And he kept trying to spoon me. And every time I tried to move away, he would just follow me across the bed.

jokes.text: Blake's also a guy's name, so he may be spending his afternoons with a guy named Blake. So nothing to worry about.
jokes.setup: Angela worried about Senator's yoga instructor Blake
jokes.punchline: Oscar's attempt to reassure her accidentally suggests gay affair
jokes.explanation: Oscar trying to deflect suspicion while accidentally making it worse. Great dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Angela, Blake is just a yoga instructor. There's nothing going on between him and the Senator.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: How can you be so sure?

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Because I know Blake. We go to the same gym, we shower together, we've even shared a steam room. He's completely trustworthy.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: You just walked into her office... and begin. - Hello. - Hello. May I please speak to your boss? - No, she is the boss. - I am? Yes.
jokes.setup: Role-playing sales scenario with Dwight
jokes.punchline: Dwight immediately assumes woman can't be in charge
jokes.explanation: Perfectly demonstrates Dwight's sexism in a believable way. Everyone's confusion adds to the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A woman in a position of power? That's not how the corporate hierarchy works.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Dwight, I'm literally just playing a client.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Exactly. You should be playing the secretary.

jokes.text: no one would ever believe that she would be a boss.
jokes.setup: Phyllis struggling to play the boss role
jokes.punchline: Dwight's casual sexism about Phyllis specifically
jokes.explanation: Continues the cringe but not particularly clever. Just Dwight being awful.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Phyllis as boss? That's ridiculous. Women are naturally suited for administrative roles, not leadership. She'll probably just cry when things get difficult or spend all day talking about her feelings.

jokes.text: I will tell her what her needs are and then fill them. This is gonna work out best for you if you just relax and do nothing. And once I'm finished, it's over.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining his sales approach
jokes.punchline: Sounds disturbingly like describing sexual assault
jokes.explanation: Extremely uncomfortable double entendre that Dwight is oblivious to. High cringe factor.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I use a series of calculated moves to break down a client's defenses, establish dominance, and force them into submission until they have no choice but to accept my terms.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Sounds disturbingly like describing sexual assault.

jokes.text: Have you ever killed a woman? How many women have you killed? Please, sir, will you not kill me?
jokes.setup: Jim offering feedback on Dwight's sales approach
jokes.punchline: Escalates to absurd murder accusations
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that captures how threatening Dwight's approach sounds. Great structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, your sales technique is too aggressive.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Aggressive? I'm being professional.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You literally threatened to kill that client.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That was a metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You said, 'I will murder your family.'

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: In the context of competitive sales!

jokes.text: Yeah, it's sounding kind of spooky-sexy over here, Halpert.
jokes.setup: Jim trying to take business call while Dwight training session happens
jokes.punchline: Conference call participant commenting on the background
jokes.explanation: Good reaction to Dwight's creepy sales talk. 'Spooky-sexy' is a memorable phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey, sorry about that. I'm in the middle of a training session.

dialogue.speaker: Conference Call Participant
dialogue.line: Is that a guy doing karate in the background?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It's not karate, it's Schrute Farms self-defense technique.

jokes.text: We're trying to reach Jim Halpert, not Batman.
jokes.setup: Jim's voice sounds different due to location change
jokes.punchline: Suggests Jim sounds like Batman
jokes.explanation: Simple observational joke about Jim's voice distortion. Competent but not special.

dialogue.speaker: Colin
dialogue.line: Jim, your voice sounds different. Did you move to a new location?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm calling from the warehouse now.

dialogue.speaker: Colin
dialogue.line: Well, you sound like Batman.

jokes.text: some organisms have external hairs that vibrate to indicate auditory stimulation, but unfortunately, our external hairs don't vibrate at all.
jokes.setup: Trying to explain how to show he's listening
jokes.punchline: Overcomplicated biological explanation for simple nodding
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight overcomplication of simple concepts. Loses points for verbosity but gains for originality.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: When someone is talking to you, you have to show them you're listening. It's simple.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You nod. Like this. See? The nodding motion stimulates the vestibular system in your inner ear, which sends signals to your brain confirming that you are, in fact, conscious and aware.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It's basic biology.

jokes.text: children of the world coming together, cutting down trees to make paper. But not in a child labor-y way.
jokes.setup: Nellie describing her mural concept
jokes.punchline: Accidentally describes child labor while trying to avoid it
jokes.explanation: Great example of trying to avoid a problem while creating it. Classic Nellie obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: It's a mural of workers, but not exploited workers. They're happy workers. They're singing while they work. And they're all... they're all very young.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: No, no, no—they chose to be there. It's not forced labor. They get breaks. Short breaks, but breaks.

jokes.text: he looks like he's laboring over a stool, having just eaten human flesh.
jokes.setup: Describing Dwight's listening face
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific and grotesque description
jokes.explanation: Incredibly vivid and disturbing imagery that's both funny and memorable. Peak Nellie weirdness.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: He has this listening face... it's like he's concentrating so hard that his face gets all scrunched up, and his eyes go really wide, and his mouth hangs open just slightly, like he's trying to suck the words directly into his brain through his face.

jokes.text: I carried each one of them for nine months inside of my torso and then pushed them out of my vagina.
jokes.setup: Dwight role-playing as a woman discussing her kids
jokes.punchline: Overly clinical and inappropriate description of childbirth
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Dwight's complete lack of social awareness. Uncomfortable in exactly the right way.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My children were delivered vaginally at 37 weeks gestation. The perineal tearing was extensive but manageable. I hemorrhaged approximately 800 milliliters but remained conscious throughout. Both infants scored 9 on the Apgar scale.

jokes.text: I used to watch Pulp Fiction and laugh, and now I'm like, 'that poor gimp is somebody's child.'
jokes.setup: Jim explaining how having kids makes you soft
jokes.punchline: Specific example of feeling sympathy for movie villain
jokes.explanation: Relatable parental humor with a specific, unexpected reference. Good character development.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, having kids really does make you soft. Like, I was watching this movie the other day, and there was this villain, right? And I found myself actually feeling bad for him. I was like, 'Man, he probably just had a rough childhood or something.'

jokes.text: I had a barber once who used to comb my hair gently.
jokes.setup: Asked to think of a nice, reasonable person
jokes.punchline: Dwight's surprisingly tender memory
jokes.explanation: Unexpectedly sweet moment that shows Dwight's vulnerability. Great character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of my mother. She was the most beautiful woman in the world. She had these soft hands, and she would brush my hair when I was sick. And she smelled like... like cinnamon and old money.

jokes.text: He used to fight dogs. Like, he used to make dogs fight, or he actually fought dogs? Little of this, little of that.
jokes.setup: Dwight's fond memory of his gentle barber
jokes.punchline: Reveals the barber was involved in dog fighting
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark turn that subverts the sweet moment. 'Little of this, little of that' is perfectly Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My barber was a gentle man. He'd trim my hair with such care and precision. I trusted him completely.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's nice, Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yeah. Of course, he's currently serving eight to ten years for dog fighting.

jokes.text: They're all fatties.
jokes.setup: Looking for yoga instructor Blake
jokes.punchline: Angela's judgmental comment about yoga class
jokes.explanation: In character for Angela but not particularly clever. Just mean-spirited judgment.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm looking for Blake, my yoga instructor. I hope he's not one of those people who does yoga for the exercise. Real yoga is about spiritual enlightenment, not getting a flat stomach.

jokes.text: dog style.
jokes.setup: Watching downward-facing dog yoga pose
jokes.punchline: Oscar's awkward reference to doggy style
jokes.explanation: Obvious sexual pun that's below Oscar's usual wit. Feels forced.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: That's the downward-facing dog pose.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Not to be confused with doggy style.

jokes.text: I'd like to see that run for office.
jokes.setup: Seeing Blake's boyfriend with a ponytail
jokes.punchline: Angela's conservative judgment about appearance and electability
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Angela's political conservatism and judgment

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: A ponytail? Really? I'm sorry, but a man with a ponytail cannot be taken seriously. How is he ever going to get elected to office looking like that?

jokes.text: Hey! Are those skateboarders back? Where are they? Little punks.
jokes.setup: Jim trying to have professional call outside
jokes.punchline: Neighbor thinks Jim's conference call noise is from skateboarders
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation of Jim's call problems. Neighbor character adds to the chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yes, I understand. We need to finalize the quarterly projections by Friday.

dialogue.speaker: Jim's neighbor
dialogue.line: Hey! Tell those skateboarders to keep it down!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I'm on a conference call.

dialogue.speaker: Jim's neighbor
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, so am I! And I can't hear myself think over all that noise!

jokes.text: Oh, dear God in heaven.
jokes.setup: Dwight realizes his client is Jan
jokes.punchline: Dwight's rare moment of genuine fear
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for Jan reveal. Dwight rarely shows fear, making this impactful.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jan? Jan Levinson?

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Hello, Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I... I didn't know you were coming. Michael didn't mention...

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: He doesn't know I'm here. This is a surprise inspection.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Oh God.

jokes.text: Jan used to be one of my superiors, and she is one of the most erratic and terrifying people I have ever met.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining who Jan is
jokes.punchline: Dwight calling someone else erratic when he's the office weirdo
jokes.explanation: Ironic coming from Dwight. Good callback to Jan's history of crazy behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jan is Michael's girlfriend. She's very erratic and unpredictable.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, you're talking about someone being erratic?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What? I'm perfectly normal.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You sleep under your desk and wear a militia outfit to work.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's called being prepared.

jokes.text: Your daughter could be a bubble-bath model. I could just bite her head off.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to compliment Jan's daughter
jokes.punchline: Extremely creepy phrasing that sounds threatening
jokes.explanation: Peak cringe Dwight. Trying to be nice but sounds like a predator. Perfectly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Your daughter has really filled out. I've been watching her development very closely.

jokes.text: I just saw your face. Oh. I'm sorry. It's for the thing. I know. That's great. It just-- it makes it look like there's an eyebrow in the middle of your face.
jokes.setup: Pete's Movember mustache
jokes.punchline: Erin's honest but hurtful description of how it looks
jokes.explanation: Classic Erin honesty causing awkwardness. The eyebrow description is vivid and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: You know, Pete, I think your mustache looks really... distinctive. Like, if I saw it on a milk carton, I'd definitely call the number.

jokes.text: you look like a cyclops whose eye fell out... which is great.
jokes.setup: Erin continuing to describe Pete's mustache
jokes.punchline: Increasingly disturbing description followed by false reassurance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Erin escalation - making it worse while trying to be supportive. Great visual imagery.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: It's like a caterpillar is slowly eating his upper lip. And sometimes I think about what happens when it reaches the other side, and I have nightmares about it. But anyway, it looks great on him!

jokes.text: Jan's daughter singing song about how great her mommy is
jokes.setup: Jan showing off her daughter
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top praise song clearly coached by narcissistic mother
jokes.explanation: Classic Jan using her child for ego stroking. Uncomfortable but believable.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: This is my daughter Astrid. Go ahead, tell them what you told me.

dialogue.speaker: Astrid
dialogue.line: My mommy is the most beautiful, smartest, and most talented woman in the whole world. Everyone says so.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: She came up with that all by herself.

jokes.text: Well, it's not really much of a comparison, is it? I mean, 'Cece' is two letters, and 'Astrid' is... I mean, there's even some adults who-- who-- who can't spell it.
jokes.setup: Pam mentions her daughter Cece can spell her name
jokes.punchline: Jan's competitive put-down of a toddler's achievement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jan competitiveness - turning child development into a contest. Appropriately awful.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Oh, well, Astrid could spell her name when she was two. And use it in a sentence.

jokes.text: Try to spell it, Pam. Um... A... X? I don't-- you got me.
jokes.setup: Jan challenging Pam to spell Astrid
jokes.punchline: Pam's confused attempt at spelling
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment. Jan's pettiness and Pam's bewilderment work well together.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Spell Astrid.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: A-S-T-R-I-D?

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: That's correct.

jokes.text: You thought I had no more cards left to play. Well, I've got one. Man-boy! The ace of babes.
jokes.setup: Dwight's plan to win over Jan
jokes.punchline: Calling Ryan his secret weapon with ridiculous nicknames
jokes.explanation: 'Ace of babes' is a great Dwight-ism. Shows his weird understanding of sexuality and power.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Ryan is my secret weapon. I'm going to call him 'The Ryan' from now on. Actually, no—'Ryantiago.' No wait, 'Ryan Gosling.' No, that's taken. How about 'The Ryanator'? 'Ryan McAwesome'? 'Ryan Seacrest.' I'm going with 'Ryan Seacrest.'

jokes.text: Where's the quiznos? You're the quiznos.
jokes.setup: Ryan expecting to be taken to a restaurant
jokes.punchline: Dwight's metaphorical explanation that Ryan IS the product
jokes.explanation: Absurd but somehow makes sense in Dwight's mind. Ryan's confusion is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: So where are we going? That new steakhouse downtown?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Ryan, you ARE the product. I'm not taking you to a restaurant. I'm taking you to be sold.

jokes.text: Do you have a valid passport?
jokes.setup: Jan considering Ryan as her liaison
jokes.punchline: Ominous question suggesting international travel/trafficking
jokes.explanation: Perfectly ominous Jan moment. Simple question that implies terrible things.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Ryan would be perfect as my liaison.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's great, Jan. Where are you thinking of taking him?

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Eastern Europe.

jokes.text: 415-YCL. That's a license number? That's all you need.
jokes.setup: Pam suggesting Dwight get Molly's number
jokes.punchline: Dwight thinks license plate number is sufficient
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight logic - completely missing how dating works. Original and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: You should get Molly's number.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have her license plate number. That's all I need.

jokes.text: women love gossip, it's like * you people.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining why Pam should help with Molly
jokes.punchline: Casual sexism delivered as if it's helpful
jokes.explanation: Standard Dwight sexism. In character but not particularly clever or original.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Pam, you're a woman. You should help with Molly. It's what you're naturally inclined to do.

tmdb_overview: The women of the office try to teach Dwight how to interact with female clients; Angela suspects the senator of cheating; Toby convinces some of the guys to grow mustaches.
insight: Late-season character comedy sustains 79.9 on cringe alone—no standout laughs needed.
